q: Which command is used to change file permissions?
exp: chmod (change mode) is used to modify file/directory permissions. chown changes ownership.

q: What is a daemon?
exp: A daemon is a background process that runs without user interaction, like web servers or database services.

q: What does the permission '755' mean in Linux?
opts: Full for owner / Read-Exec for others
exp: 755 means: owner=read+write+execute (7), group=read+execute (5), others=read+execute (5). Common for executable files.

q: In a headless environment, how are applications and system configurations primarily managed?
opts: By physically plugging in a monitor
exp: Headless servers have no GUI, so all management is done via CLI commands over SSH or other text-based protocols.

q: Which command displays the last few lines of a file?
exp: tail shows the end of a file (default 10 lines). head shows the beginning. cat displays the entire file.

q: What resources are considered most when a file server is being setup?
exp: File servers need high CPU for handling multiple file requests and large storage capacity for file storage.

q: What is the primary characteristic of a 'headless' server?
opts: It can only run on Windows
It is a server that has crashed
It operates without a graphical user interface (GUI), monitor, keyboard, or mouse
exp: A headless server is a computer without GUI, monitor, or keyboard—managed entirely via command-line or network.

q: Which command allows you to view running processes?
exp: ps lists currently running processes. top shows an interactive view. ls lists files, not processes.

q: What command shows disk space usage?
exp: df (disk free) shows available disk space on mounted filesystems. du shows directory/file size usage.

q: In Linux, what is the purpose of the 'etc' directory?
exp: /etc stores system configuration files like networking, user accounts, and application settings.

q: Which command is used to copy files or directories?
exp: cp copies files/directories. mv moves/renames them. rm deletes. ln creates links.

q: Which command can be used to display the last lines of a file?
exp: tail displays the last lines of a file. 'last' shows login history, not file content.

q: What is the default port for SSH?
exp: SSH uses port 22 by default. Port 80 is HTTP, 443 is HTTPS, 21 is FTP.

q: What is a key advantage of using a headless server configuration?
opts: Reduced resource overhead, allowing more power for server applications
exp: Without GUI overhead, headless servers use fewer resources—more CPU/RAM available for applications.

q: How do you allow a port through the UFW firewall?
exp: ufw allow opens specific ports. 'ufw enable' turns on the firewall, 'ufw open' and 'ufw accept' are not valid commands.

q: Which command shows disk usage?
exp: df shows disk space available. du shows directory/file disk usage.

q: What is a 'headless' server?
exp: A headless server has no monitor, keyboard, or mouse—managed remotely via network/CLI only.

q: Which user has the highest administrative privileges?
exp: root is the superuser with full administrative privileges on Unix/Linux systems.

q: What is the minimum RAM for a basic web server?
exp: 512MB is typically the minimum for basic web server operations like Apache with modest traffic.

q: Which command renames a file?
exp: mv (move) renames files and directories. 'rename' and 'rn' don't exist as default commands in most shells.

q: What command is used to create a new directory?
exp: mkdir (make directory) creates new directories. 'newdir' and 'crdir' are not valid commands.

q: How do you check your current IP address?
exp: Both 'ip addr' (modern) and 'ifconfig' (legacy) display network interfaces and IP addresses.

q: Which command is used to copy files in Linux?
exp: cp copies files/directories. mv moves them. 'copy' and 'xcopy' are Windows commands.

q: Which protocol is most commonly used for headless server management?
exp: SSH (Secure Shell) is the standard for securely managing headless Linux servers remotely.

q: What is a static IP address?
exp: A static IP is manually configured and remains the same. A dynamic IP is assigned by DHCP and may change.

q: What is a package repository?
exp: A repository is a remote server storing software packages for installation (e.g., Ubuntu's main, universe).

q: Which command would you use to change to the root user?
exp: su (substitute user) switches to root or another user. sudo runs commands with elevated privileges.

q: Which command enables UFW?
exp: ufw enable turns on the firewall. 'ufw on' and 'ufw run' are not valid UFW commands.

q: Which command is used to search for a specific text string in a file?
exp: grep searches for text patterns in files. find locates files by name. 'search' is not a standard command.

q: Which command shows the current directory?
exp: pwd (print working directory) shows the current directory path. 'where' and 'showdir' are not standard Linux commands.

q: What command is used to manage user accounts in Linux?
exp: useradd, adduser, and usermod all manage user accounts. useradd is low-level, adduser is more interactive.

q: What command shows the manual for a command?
exp: man (manual) displays documentation for commands. 'help' is for shell built-ins, 'guide' and 'info' are not standard.

q: Which Linux file is responsible for APT tools main configuration?
exp: /etc/apt/apt.conf stores main APT configuration options for package management in Debian-based systems.

q: Which command creates a new user?
exp: adduser interactively creates a new user with home directory and shell. 'makeuser' and 'newuser' don't exist.

q: What is the minimum requirement for CPU in an Ubuntu Server?
exp: 1.0GHz is the official minimum CPU requirement for Ubuntu Server. Most modern systems exceed this.

q: Which command shuts down the system?
exp: Both 'poweroff' and 'shutdown' can shut down a system. 'shutdown -h now' is most common and allows scheduling.

q: Who is the creator of the Linux kernel?
exp: Linus Torvalds created the Linux kernel in 1991. Stallman created GNU, Jobs created macOS, Gates created Windows.

q: Which command shows network connection details including port numbers?
exp: netstat displays active network connections, listening ports, and protocol statistics.

q: What does SSH stand for?
exp: SSH = Secure Shell. It's a cryptographic protocol for secure remote command execution over a network.

q: Which linux command shows the username associated with the current effective user ID?
exp: whoami displays the current effective username. 'who' shows logged-in users, 'pwd' shows directory, 'echo' prints strings.

q: Which of the following scenarios is ideal for a headless server?
opts: A 24/7 file server or database server
exp: Headless servers excel at running 24/7 without user interaction—perfect for file/database servers, not for gaming/design.

q: What resources are considered most when a web server is being setup?
exp: Web servers need CPU for processing requests and RAM for caching. Storage is less critical than for file servers.

q: What is a service?
opts: Both a running process and background
exp: A service is a process running in the background (daemon) providing functionality to the system or other processes.

q: What is the purpose of the 'kill' command?
exp: kill sends signals to processes to terminate them. For restarting, use 'systemctl restart' or 'kill -9' as last resort.

q: What is the root user?
exp: root is the superuser account with UID 0, having full system privileges for administration.

q: What is the minimum requirement for RAM in an Ubuntu Server?
exp: 1.0GB is the official minimum RAM for Ubuntu Server. 2GB+ is recommended for comfortable operation.

q: What is a firewall used for?
exp: A firewall controls incoming/outgoing network traffic based on rules to protect against unauthorized access.

q: Cockpit is a ....
exp: Cockpit is a web-based interface for managing Linux servers—can monitor systems, manage users, and containers.

q: What is Ubuntu Server primarily used for?
exp: Ubuntu Server is designed for hosting web services, databases, and applications—not for desktop tasks.

q: Which protocol is most commonly used to remotely manage a headless Linux server?
exp: SSH is the standard protocol for secure remote command-line access. SMTP is for email, FTP for file transfer, RDP for Windows.

q: Which command is used to connect to a remote server via SSH?
exp: ssh username@hostname establishes a secure connection to a remote server via SSH protocol.
